lines.text: IN REPLY
REFER TO:
HEADQUARTERS
AIR DEFENSE COMMAND
MITCHEL FIELD, NEW YORK
15 September 1947
Classification (cancelled) [changed to [REDACTED]]
effective on [REDACTED]
under the authority of AC/S, G-2
SUBJECT: Unidentified Flying Objects
SUMMARY OF INFORMATION:
The following information relative to the sighting of a possible
flying disc 4 August 1947, was received 10 September 1947 from [REDACTED]
Jackson Heights, New York.
On 4 August 1947, [REDACTED] Pan American Airways Inc., was the
navigator of a Constellation type aircraft on a flight from Gander, New-
foundland to La Guardia Field, New York. At 1600, at a position approxi-
mately 10 miles NW of Boston, Mass., [REDACTED] sighted a flying object which
he was unable to identify. At this time [REDACTED] was sitting in the co-
pilots seat, and looking out the right side for other aircraft that might
be in the vicinity. [REDACTED] stated that when he first sighted the object,
it appeared to be about 3 miles away, and at least 1,000 feet below the level
of the Constellation. [REDACTED] believes that he studied the object for almost
30 seconds before he called the pilot's attention to it. [REDACTED] described
the object as being a drop gold in color, and that its' surface reflected
light. It appeared to be about 13 feet in length, and from 2-3 feet in depth;
elliptical in shape and blunted at the ends. [REDACTED] estimated the speed of
the object as being 175 miles per hour, and that it was travelling in an east-
erly direction, on a course of approximately 110° magnetic. [REDACTED] then
told the aircraft commander that there was an unidentified object to the right,
and again glanced out to observe the object, catching a momentary glimpse of
it before the pilot banked the plane to the left, then the aircraft banked
[REDACTED] lost sight of the object. At this point, [REDACTED] the aircraft
Captain, exclaimed that he was able to see a similar object on his side.
[REDACTED] was unable to see the object on [REDACTED] side. He stated that
he does not believe that it was the same object that he had viewed inasmuch
as the one he saw if it had remained on the same course would have been
hidden from view by that time by the wings and tail section of the Constella-
tion. [REDACTED] stated that the pilot then righted the plane. The entire in-
cident transpired in less than a minute and one-half.
[REDACTED] describes the weather condition at the time of sighting as
follows: about 5/10th scattered cumulus, with tops at 10,000 feet; visibility
10 miles; wind at flight level, West at about 15 mph. The flight level of the
aircraft at this time was 8,000 feet; TAS 270, and the magnetic course was 247°.
AGENTS NOTES:
[REDACTED] has been employed by Pan American Airways
for the past [REDACTED] as a Navigator, and during the war worked with PAA
